name: Amulet of the Planes
current: dndType=equipment, sub=light (armor), lootType=loot.armor.magic, folder=armor/magic-armor-shields/light-armor, system.type.value="light"
suggested: Re-tag as magic equipment (lootType loot.equipment.magic), set armor subtype to a non-armor value (trinket/clothing) so it stops granting a base AC, and move out of the armor/light-armor folder into a wondrous/magic-equipment folder.
rationale: Its own description reads "Wondrous Item (requires attunement)" — it is a neck amulet, not light armor; carrying armor subtype "light" wrongly gives it an armor AC base and files it as armor in loot/merchant filters.

verdict.note: Verified against the real item JSON (line 115 of packs/infinity-dnd5e-items.db, _id 8ABk0XV76Hzq8Qul). Every "current"-state claim checks out: type=equipment with system.type.value="light" (armor "light" subtype), flags.infinity-dnd5e.lootType="loot.armor.magic", merchantCategories include "armor", and folder = armor/magic-armor-shields/light-armor (folderPathKey + folder object both confirm). The rationale is also confirmed: the description literally reads "Wondrous Item (requires attunement)", the item-state list says "Equipment, Jewelry", and img is a neck pendant (icons/equipment/neck/pendant-faceted-green.webp). The bad armor classification has even leaked into player-facing text — the unidentified description reads "A jewelry armor awaiting identification... Visible form: Jewelry Armor." In dnd5e, equipment with type.value="light" is treated as wearable Light Armor (occupies the armor AC slot / proficiency path) even though system.armor.value is 0, so the tag is functionally wrong, not cosmetic. This is NOT an alias (loot.armor.magic is the stored lootType, and the armor TYPE is the root cause), and NOT systemic: a scoped scan found this is the only non-armor neck/ring/cloak item carrying an armor type.value (the other "Ring Mail" hits are genuine heavy armor). Item-specific and actionable.
verdict.refinedSuggestion: Re-type as a true wondrous/jewelry item: change system.type.value from "light" to a non-armor value (e.g. "trinket" or clear it) so it no longer occupies the Light Armor slot or grants an armor AC base. Re-derive the loot tags accordingly: lootType loot.equipment.magic (drop loot.armor.magic), remove "armor" from merchantCategories/keywords (keep equipment/magic/outfitting), drop subtype.light / folder.family.armor / folder.leaf.light-armor keywords, and move it from armor/magic-armor-shields/light-armor into a wondrous/magic-equipment folder. Also regenerate the unidentified description so it stops calling the amulet "Jewelry Armor." Root cause is upstream system.type.value="light" driving the armor folder + lootType derivation, so fixing the type and re-running the tagger should cascade correctly.

name: Black Opal
issueType: tier/value inversion (rarity-derived tier ignores gp) across gemstones
current: 12 gemstones at 250-1000gp (valueBand v3/v4) are tagged tier t1 because rarity is empty, while cheaper gems with a rarity get higher tiers: Black Opal 1000gp t1, Cathedral Ruby (8rEeTCElX7x4CKgQ) 1000gp t1, Dragon Garnet (bx5reodItf5nVWjr) 1000gp t1, Royal Alexandrite Shard (z23R1KnpLep8zFHm) 1000gp t1, Black Pearl Pair (AvZNeZWMYvvq65Hb) 500gp t1, Ember Spinel (UknNCLG0V2shkXTI) 500gp t1, Emerald (2q1Cvb7GDga4dq13) 500gp t1, Imperial Topaz (eYWbGHHhTVTZhis1) 500gp t1, Mist-Vein Emerald (9hLppZ9P8ddKWCzn) 500gp t1, Fire Opal Tear (vY5nszZ6U14X8Vrg) 250gp t1, Queen's Tear Pearl (eqahThWz5W5jlRxD) 250gp t1, Sapphire (MgBcW83MBYjAbuLZ) 250gp t1 -- yet Alexandrite 200gp = t2 and Diamond 150gp = t2
suggested: Derive gem tier from value band (gp) rather than rarity, so a 1000gp gem ranks at/above a 150gp gem. Expected ~t3 for the v3 500gp gems and ~t4 for the v4 1000gp gems within this folder's curve
rationale: Within the same gemstones folder, 1000gp stones rank tier t1 while 150-200gp stones rank t2 -- a value/tier inversion that mis-sorts the most valuable gems to the lowest tier.

verdict.note: Verified against pack data E:/Infinity D&D5e/.claude/worktrees/cranky-proskuriakova-236245/packs/infinity-dnd5e-items.db. Black Opal (0BIXYULR7r7Uguhv): gpValue=1000, valueBand=value.v4, rarityNormalized="" (empty), tier=tier.t1 — exactly as the finding claims. All 12 cited gems confirmed (250-1000gp, empty rarity, all t1). Counter-examples confirmed: alexandrite (200gp, rarity=uncommon -> t2) and diamond (150gp, rarity=uncommon -> t2). Enumerated the whole gemstones folder (38 items): tier.t1 spans gp 5-5000; tier.t2 spans gp 25-250. Every single t2 gem has rarityNormalized=uncommon and every t1 gem has (none)/common — tier tracks rarity, not price. The extreme cases: 5000gp winterglass-diamond and 1000gp black-opal/cathedral-ruby/dragon-garnet/royal-alexandrite-shard are t1, while 25gp diamond-dust is t2. This is a genuine, item-specific value/tier inversion, not an alias and not a duplicate-systemic noise tag. It is actionable: hoard-loot.js line 515 feeds tier into tierWindow() to filter the candidate pool, so a high-tier loot roll would surface the 25gp t2 dust but exclude the 1000-5000gp t1 gems — misrouting the most valuable treasure away from high-tier encounters. Caveat on the suggestion: the codebase's TIERS are an APL/character-power band (tag-vocabulary.js line 24: t1=lvl1-4 ... t5=epic), and a separate gp-driven valueBand (v1-v5) already exists; the gem tier currently defaults to t1 whenever rarity is empty, which is the root cause.
verdict.refinedSuggestion: For treasure with no inherent power level (gemstones/art in the sundries/gems-jewelry/gemstones folder), stop defaulting tier to t1 when rarity is empty. Instead derive the gem's tier from its gpValue/valueBand so price monotonically maps to tier: e.g. map value bands to tiers (roughly v1/v2->t1, v3 (250-500gp)->t2/t3, v4 (1000gp)->t4, v5 (5000gp)->t5), or at minimum ensure a 1000gp gem ranks >= a 150-200gp uncommon gem. This is a re-tag of the pack data (schema po-loot-v3, tier field + tier.tN keyword) — the empty-rarity fallback in the upstream tagging pipeline is the bug. Concretely, black-opal/cathedral-ruby/dragon-garnet/royal-alexandrite-shard/star-sapphire-cabochon (1000gp v4) should land around t4, the 500gp v3 gems around t3, and they must not sit below alexandrite/diamond (150-200gp uncommon, currently t2). Verified the anomaly is unique: of 319 spells, 298 use ["arcana","magic","spell"] and the other ~20 only add minor specialty cats (curio/travel/herb/jewelry); Spirit Guardians is the SOLE spell with art/loot/luxury/treasure cats, the SOLE spell with saleLiquidity "sale.luxury" (236 standard / 82 specialty / 1 luxury), and the SOLE spell with variableTreasureKind "art". This is not an alias and not a value rule — 250,000 gp spells like Wish/Time Stop are plain ["arcana","magic","spell"] with sale.specialty, so a 500 gp spell carrying art/treasure/luxury tags is clear item-specific data pollution that would mis-stock it with art/treasure merchants and mis-price it via luxury liquidity. Real, actionable, item-specific.
verdict.refinedSuggestion: Strip the art/treasure pollution and align Spirit Guardians to the standard spell-source profile: set merchantCategories to ["arcana","magic","spell"], saleLiquidity to "sale.standard" (matches the 236-spell majority for a low-value 500 gp / tier.t2 spell), remove variableTreasureKind ("art") and lootType/loot.variable.art treasure framing, and drop the polluting keywords (merchant.art, merchant.loot, merchant.luxury, merchant.treasure, treasure.art, loot.variable, loot.variable.art, sale.luxury), keeping merchant.arcana/merchant.magic/merchant.spell and sale.standard. Fix in the source packs/*.db record, then recompile via npm run compile:packs.
